title: Inventory & Skew
subtitle: When position should move your quote
intro: Every fill changes your inventory — how many units you are long or short. If you buy repeatedly, you accumulate long inventory; your risk rises if the fair value falls. Market makers skew quotes to offload unwanted position.

Skew means shifting your bid and ask together (or asymmetrically) to encourage trades that reduce inventory. Too long? Lower both bid and ask so buyers are less eager but sellers hit your bid less — or tighten the ask to attract buyers who lift you. The exact style varies, but the goal is the same: get flat without giving away edge.
In interview games you often face position limits. Hitting the limit may force you to quote one-sided or widen dramatically. Ignoring inventory is a common way to blow up in mock trading.
This lesson connects position to quote adjustments — the bridge between fair value math and practical market making.

Harris describes inventory risk as the chance that prices move while you hold position. In a fast interview game you might go long three contracts in thirty seconds — your quote should stop looking symmetric around abstract fair and start pulling you flat.

Skew is not panic. You shift prices to make the relieving trade attractive: if you are long, you might shade both sides down slightly but tighten your ask to invite buyers, or widen your bid so you stop accumulating. The art is moving enough to reduce risk without donating edge on every fill.

primer.heading: Inventory: long, short, flat
body: **Inventory** is your net position. Every fill changes it: buying makes you **long** (you own units), selling makes you **short** (you owe units), and a matched buy-and-sell leaves you **flat** (zero).
Market makers want to earn the spread, not bet on direction — so inventory is a by-product to be managed, not a position to fall in love with.

keyTerms.definition: Net positive position — you bought more than you sold.
Net negative position — you sold more than you bought.
Zero net position; no directional risk.

visual.caption: How a fill moves your position

primer.heading: Inventory risk
body: Holding inventory exposes you to price moves before you can flatten. A **long** loses when fair value **falls**; a **short** loses when fair value **rises**.
This is why a big position should pull your quote toward getting flat — symmetric quoting around abstract fair ignores the risk you are actually carrying.

keyTerms.definition: The chance that fair value moves against your held position before you flatten.

visual.title: Which way hurts
visual.text: Long loses if fair drops; short loses if fair rises. The bigger the position, the more a move costs you.

primer.heading: Skew: shift quotes to get flat
body: **Skew** means moving your bid and ask to make the *relieving* trade more attractive. If you are **long**, shade down and keep a relatively tight ask so buyers lift you and take your inventory; make your bid less attractive so you stop accumulating.
If you are **short**, do the mirror image: shade up with a relatively tight bid so sellers hit you and you cover. Skew enough to reduce risk without donating edge on every fill.

keyTerms.definition: Shifting bid/ask relative to fair to attract the trade that reduces your inventory.

example.prompt: Fair is `100`, you are long 6. You move from `99 at 101` to `98 at 100` (same spread). What does this do?
steps: The ask falls from `101` to `100`, so buyers lift you more readily.
example.result: Cheaper offer helps you sell off the long; the lower bid means you accumulate less.

primer.heading: Position limits
body: Interview games cap how long or short you may go — a **position limit** like `±4`. At the limit you cannot add in that direction.
At max long, stop bidding and quote an **offer only** until you reduce; at max short, stop offering and bid only. Quoting normally at the limit invites a fill that would breach it.

keyTerms.definition: The maximum long or short you are allowed to hold, e.g. `±4` units.

visual.title: At the limit, go one-sided
visual.text: Max long → offer only (sell to flatten). Max short → bid only (buy to flatten). Never quote the side that would breach the cap.

primer.heading: Mark-to-market on inventory
body: Your open position is valued at current fair: **mark-to-market P&L = (fair − average cost) × quantity**. This moves with fair value even before you trade out.
Use the average cost across all your fills, not the first price you paid. End-of-game inventory is marked at the settlement fair, so flatten before the bell if you can.

example.prompt: You bought 3 units at an average of `20`. Fair value is now `17`. Mark-to-market P&L?
example.result: Loss of `$9` on the open inventory.

visual.caption: Positive when fair beats your cost on a long; negative when it moves against you.

primerNarration.title: Long, short, or flat
primerNarration.caption: Inventory is your net position. Every fill changes it. Buying makes you long, selling makes you short, and a matched buy and sell leaves you flat. Market makers want to earn the spread, not bet on direction, so inventory is a by-product to manage.
bullets: Long means you bought more than you sold
Short means you sold more than you bought

primerNarration.title: Which way hurts
primerNarration.caption: Holding inventory exposes you to price moves before you can flatten. A long position loses when fair value falls. A short position loses when fair value rises. The bigger the position, the more a move costs you.

visual.text: Long loses if fair drops. Short loses if fair rises.

primerNarration.title: Shift quotes to get flat
primerNarration.caption: Skew means moving your bid and ask to make the relieving trade attractive. If you are long, shade down and keep a relatively tight ask so buyers take your inventory. If you are short, shade up with a tight bid so sellers let you cover. Skew enough to cut risk without giving away edge.

primerNarration.title: At the limit, go one-sided
primerNarration.caption: Games cap how long or short you may go. At the maximum long you cannot buy more, so stop bidding and offer only until you reduce. At the maximum short, bid only. Quoting normally at the limit invites a fill that would breach it.

visual.text: Max long, offer only. Max short, bid only.

primerNarration.title: Value the open position
primerNarration.caption: Your open position is valued at current fair. Mark-to-market profit equals fair minus your average cost, times quantity. It moves with fair value even before you trade out. Buying three units at twenty when fair drops to seventeen is a nine-dollar loss on inventory.

questions.question: Fair value is 50. You are long 10 units (you own too many). To reduce length, you should generally:
options: Make your ask more attractive so buyers lift you
Make your bid more attractive so sellers hit you
Keep quotes unchanged — inventory does not matter
Quote only a bid, no ask

explanations.A: Correct. A more attractive (lower) ask encourages buyers to lift you — you sell inventory and reduce length.
explanations.B: A better bid brings sellers — you'd buy more and increase length.
explanations.C: Inventory absolutely matters for risk and quoting.
explanations.D: One-sided quoting is a last resort, not the first response.

remediation.question: You are long. You want buyers to lift your ask. You should make your ask:

explanations.B: Correct — cheaper ask attracts buyers who take your length.

remediation.question: Long inventory exposes you to risk if:

explanations.B: Correct — you lose if value drops while holding units.
explanations.C: Spread alone doesn't define inventory P&L.

questions.question: Fair 40. You are short 5 units (you owe the market). Best skew direction:
options: Make your bid more attractive so sellers hit you and you buy back
Ignore — short inventory has no risk

explanations.A: Correct. Higher bid (or relatively tighter bid) encourages sellers to hit you — you cover shorts.
explanations.B: Attractive ask makes you shorter when buyers lift.
explanations.C: May need bid to cover; removing forever isn't a strategy.
explanations.D: Short inventory risks rising fair value.

remediation.question: Short inventory loses if fair value:

explanations.C: Flat reduces mark-to-market change but position still binds.

remediation.question: To cover a short, you need to:

questions.question: Your limit is ±8 units. You are long 8. What is typically forced?
options: You may only quote to sell (reduce length), not buy more
You may only quote to buy

explanations.A: Correct. At max long, you cannot take more length — often one-sided or skewed to sell.
explanations.B: Buying adds length — forbidden at limit.

remediation.question: At max short −10, you should avoid:

explanations.A: Correct — selling increases short past limit.
explanations.C: You may still need an ask to manage.

remediation.question: Position limits exist to:

questions.question: Fair 100, flat inventory. You become long 6. You shift quote from 99–101 to 98–100 (same spread). This skew:
options: Lowers your ask, making it easier for buyers to lift you

explanations.A: Correct. Midpoint moves from 100 to 99 and ask drops from 101 to 100 — cheaper offer helps offload long inventory.

remediation.question: Skewing a quote means:
options: Moving bid/ask relative to fair to manage inventory

explanations.B: Width change alone isn't full skew story.

remediation.question: Flat inventory, fair 50. Ideal quote midpoint is:

explanations.B: Correct — center on fair when flat.

questions.question: You bought 3 units at average 20. Fair value is now 17. Mark-to-market loss on inventory (ignoring spread earned) is:

remediation.question: Long 2 at 10, fair now 14. MTM gain:
Inventory P&L moves with:

questions.question: Near end of a trading game, you are long 7 with limit 8. Fair unchanged. Priority should be:
options: Maximize spread capture on new round-trips
Flatten before the bell — reduce length even if you give up some edge

explanations.A: Adding length near limit/end is dangerous.
explanations.B: Correct. End-game risk — inventory marks against you; flattening often dominates.
explanations.D: May not be allowed; flattening is active choice.

remediation.question: End-of-session large inventory is risky because:
options: You may be marked at settlement fair with no time to trade out

remediation.question: When flat, primary edge source is usually:

explanations.B: Inventory is risk, not primary edge when flat.

questions.question: Fair is 50. You are long 5 contracts (at your position limit). You want to reduce inventory without crossing fair by much. Which quote best reflects standard skew?
options: 49 at 50 (lower both sides; tighter ask to attract buyers)
48 at 52 (wide and low — discourage all trading)

explanations.A: Correct. Long inventory → encourage sells to you less and buys from you more; shading down with a relatively tight ask invites lift-offs.
explanations.B: 48 at 52 widens but still centers low; discouraging all flow is slow when you need to flatten.
explanations.C: Raising both sides encourages more long accumulation — wrong direction.
explanations.D: Symmetric widen ignores directional inventory pressure.

remediation.question: Too long. You want buyers to lift you. Ask should be:

explanations.A: Correct — make selling to the market easy for you.
explanations.D: Bid = ask is zero spread, unusual.

remediation.question: Long inventory → bid should generally be:

questions.question: Fair is 100. You are short 3 contracts. Which adjustment best skews toward flattening?
options: 101 at 102 — shade up, relatively tight bid to attract sellers
97 at 98 — shade down to buy cheaply

explanations.A: Correct. Short → raise quotes and make your bid attractive so sellers hit you and you cover.
explanations.B: Shading down makes your bid less attractive — you stay short longer.
explanations.C: Only widening ask does not actively pull inventory in.

remediation.question: Short inventory. You want sellers to hit your bid. Bid should be:

explanations.A: Correct — encourage incoming sells to you.

remediation.question: Short means you:
options: Sold more than you bought (net negative position)

explanations.A: Correct — net short is negative inventory.

questions.question: Position limit is ±4. You are long 4. Fair is 30. What is the most appropriate quoting rule?
options: Stop bidding — offer only (one-sided) until you reduce position
Quote 29 at 31 normally — limits don't matter

explanations.A: Correct. At max long you cannot buy more; one-sided offer lets you sell without violating limits.
explanations.B: Normal two-sided quoting would let sellers hit your bid and breach the limit.
explanations.D: Absurdly low bid is unnecessary — simply don't bid at limit.

remediation.question: At max long, you should NOT:
options: Accept trades that increase long further

explanations.A: Correct — cannot add long at limit.

remediation.question: Limit +4, position +4. Buying one more is:

questions.question: Fair is 40 and unchanged. You buy 2 at 38 (bid), then sell 1 at 42 (ask). You are now long 1 at average cost 38. Mark-to-market P&L vs fair on the open lot is:

explanations.A: Correct. Long 1 at 38 with fair 40 → unrealized gain 2 on the remaining contract (plus you already locked spread on the round-trip of one lot).
explanations.C: Zero ignores fair above your cost.

remediation.question: Long 1 at cost 38. Fair 40. Unrealized P&L:
Buy at 38, fair 40. Position is:

questions.question: Fair value is 40. You buy 3 contracts at 38, then buy 2 more at 39. Fair then falls to 36 and you still hold all 5. What is your mark-to-market P&L?

explanations.A: Correct. Total cost = 3×38 + 2×39 = 114 + 78 = 192; marked at 36 the 5 units are worth 180; P&L = 180 − 192 = −$12.
explanations.B: −$20 marks the drop from the original fair 40 down to 36 (4 × 5 units), but your cost basis is the 38.4 average you actually paid, not 40.
explanations.C: −$2.40 is the per-unit loss (36 − 38.4); you must multiply it by all 5 units.
explanations.D: +$12 reverses the sign — fair fell below your average cost, so the long position loses.

remediation.question: Long 2 units bought at 10 each. Fair is now 7. Mark-to-market P&L?

explanations.B: −$3 is the per-unit loss; multiply by the 2 units held.
explanations.C: −$14 sums the prices (7 + ... ) instead of differencing them.
explanations.D: +$6 reverses the sign; fair below cost loses on a long.

remediation.question: You buy 3 at 38 and 2 at 39. Your average cost per unit is:

explanations.B: 38.5 is the simple average of 38 and 39, ignoring that you bought more at 38.
explanations.D: 38 uses only the first price and drops the units bought at 39.
